dsci_II_project

Heather Lu

2026-03-25

Load Data

EDA

# check outcome distribution 
hist(datA$log_PFS, main = "Distribution of log_PFS", xlab = "log_PFS")

Figure 1. Distribution of log_PFS.

datA |> 
  select(age, bmi, tumor_size, lymph_nodes, Ki67) |> 
  cor() |> 
  corrplot(method = "number")

Figure 2. Correlation matrix of continuous predictors in Dataset A.

From the correlation plot, it seems that lymph_nodes and tumor_size has a moderate positive correlation r = 0.71 , which makes sense in a clinical context. Everything else seems largely uncorrelated; there is no severe multicollinearity, OLS shouldn’t be too unstable. The mild correlation between lymph_nodes and tumor_size may result in one predictor being dropped by LASSO, so elastic net might be preferred here.

Train test split + model building

# train test split
set.seed(67)
data_split <- initial_split(datA, prop = 0.8)
training_data <- training(data_split)
testing_data <- testing(data_split)

# train control object
ctrl1 <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10, savePredictions = TRUE)

OLS

set.seed(67)

ols_fit <- train(log_PFS ~.,
                 data = training_data,
                 method = "lm",
                 trControl = ctrl1)

summary(ols_fit)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = .outcome ~ ., data = dat)
## 
## Residuals:
##      Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
## -2.73934 -0.47220  0.01116  0.47967  2.69926 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
## (Intercept)        2.6949155  0.1585026  17.002  < 2e-16 ***
## age               -0.0030578  0.0021697  -1.409 0.158935    
## menopause1         0.0080870  0.0528736   0.153 0.878458    
## race2              0.0185149  0.0514667   0.360 0.719086    
## race3              0.0505343  0.0635364   0.795 0.426524    
## race4             -0.0549394  0.0550933  -0.997 0.318817    
## bmi                0.0009555  0.0038984   0.245 0.806417    
## tumor_size        -0.0683906  0.0227807  -3.002 0.002723 ** 
## tumor_grade2      -0.0725526  0.0531183  -1.366 0.172174    
## tumor_grade3      -0.1907886  0.0672457  -2.837 0.004609 ** 
## lymph_nodes       -0.0682214  0.0112258  -6.077 1.53e-09 ***
## ER1                0.2291610  0.0587077   3.903 9.88e-05 ***
## PR1               -0.0120941  0.0439853  -0.275 0.783384    
## HER21             -0.1567557  0.0441886  -3.547 0.000400 ***
## Ki67              -0.0043033  0.0022338  -1.926 0.054228 .  
## stage2             0.0914317  0.0591274   1.546 0.122220    
## stage3             0.1264253  0.1166597   1.084 0.278659    
## surgery1           0.0037932  0.0379657   0.100 0.920427    
## chemo1            -0.0074195  0.0367723  -0.202 0.840124    
## hormonal_therapy1  0.1520678  0.0451454   3.368 0.000774 ***
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
## 
## Residual standard error: 0.7051 on 1580 degrees of freedom
## Multiple R-squared:  0.2112, Adjusted R-squared:  0.2017 
## F-statistic: 22.27 on 19 and 1580 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

OLS results:

  • tumor_size: negative effect, larger tumors -> shorter PFS

  • tumor_grade3: negative, poorly differentiated tumors -> shorter PFS

  • lymph_nodes: negative, more positive nodes -> shorter PFS

  • ER1: positive, ER positive -> longer PFS

  • HER21: negative, HER2 positive -> shorter PFS

  • hormonal_therapy1: positive, hormonal therapy -> longer PFS

  • age, bmi, menopause, race, PR, stage, surgery, chemo not significant in OLS

  • Adjusted R² = 0.20 : the model explains about 20% of variance in log PFS

Ridge

set.seed(67)
ridge_fit <- train(log_PFS ~ .,
                   data = training_data,
                   method = "glmnet",
                   tuneGrid = expand.grid(alpha = 0,
                                          lambda = exp(seq(6, -4, length=100))),
                   trControl = ctrl1)
## Warning in nominalTrainWorkflow(x = x, y = y, wts = weights, info = trainInfo,
## : There were missing values in resampled performance measures.
plot(ridge_fit, xTrans = log)

ridge_fit$bestTune
##    alpha    lambda
## 19     0 0.1128362

optimal lambda is 0.125, small penalty. makes sense given we don’t have severe multicollinearity, we don’t need to shrink too much.

LASSO

set.seed(67)
lasso_fit <- train(log_PFS ~ .,
                   data = training_data,
                   method = "glmnet",
                   tuneGrid = expand.grid(alpha = 1,
                                          lambda = exp(seq(4, -10, length=100))),
                   trControl = ctrl1)
## Warning in nominalTrainWorkflow(x = x, y = y, wts = weights, info = trainInfo,
## : There were missing values in resampled performance measures.
plot(lasso_fit, xTrans = log)

lasso_fit$bestTune
##    alpha     lambda
## 41     1 0.01299183
coef(lasso_fit$finalModel, s = lasso_fit$bestTune$lambda)
## 20 x 1 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
##                   s=0.01299183
## (Intercept)        2.600416237
## age               -0.001738541
## menopause1         .          
## race2              .          
## race3              0.005695736
## race4             -0.024402590
## bmi                .          
## tumor_size        -0.047114496
## tumor_grade2       .          
## tumor_grade3      -0.104548373
## lymph_nodes       -0.062144518
## ER1                0.208395550
## PR1                .          
## HER21             -0.128160939
## Ki67              -0.005143791
## stage2             0.015762828
## stage3             .          
## surgery1           .          
## chemo1             .          
## hormonal_therapy1  0.136572696

LASSO dropped menopause, bmi, tumor_grade2, PR, stage3, surgery, chemo but kept age, race4, tumor_size, tumor_grade3, lymph_nodes, ER1, HER21, Ki67, stage2, hormonal_therapy1, race3. both lymph_nodes and tumor_size were kept, but shrunken compared to OLS.

Elastic net

set.seed(67)
enet_fit <- train(log_PFS ~ .,
                   data = training_data,
                   method = "glmnet",
                   tuneGrid = expand.grid(alpha = seq(0, 1, length = 21),
                                          lambda = exp(seq(4, -10, length=100))),
                   trControl = ctrl1)
## Warning in nominalTrainWorkflow(x = x, y = y, wts = weights, info = trainInfo,
## : There were missing values in resampled performance measures.
enet_fit$bestTune
##      alpha     lambda
## 2041     1 0.01299183

enet_fit best tuning parameters yielded alpha = 0.35, lambda = 0.0172. Model is leaning more toward ridge than lasso, which makes sense given the correlation between tumor_size and lymph_nodes. It’s using the ridge component to handle those correlated predictors.

GAM

set.seed(67)

gam_fit <- train(log_PFS ~ .,
                 data = training_data,
                 method = "gam",
                 trControl = ctrl1)

gam_fit$bestTune
##   select method
## 1  FALSE GCV.Cp
gam_fit$finalModel
## 
## Family: gaussian 
## Link function: identity 
## 
## Formula:
## .outcome ~ menopause1 + race2 + race3 + race4 + tumor_grade2 + 
##     tumor_grade3 + ER1 + PR1 + HER21 + stage2 + stage3 + surgery1 + 
##     chemo1 + hormonal_therapy1 + s(lymph_nodes) + s(tumor_size) + 
##     s(bmi) + s(Ki67) + s(age)
## 
## Estimated degrees of freedom:
## 1.44 1.38 4.32 1.00 1.00  total = 24.14 
## 
## GCV score: 0.4960992

  • Applied smooth terms s() to the continuous predictors: lymph_nodes, tumor_size, bmi, Ki67, age

  • s(Ki67) has edf = 4.32 — most nonlinear, suggesting Ki67 has a notably nonlinear relationship with log PFS

  • s(lymph_nodes) edf = 1.44 and s(tumor_size) edf = 1.38 — close to 1, meaning nearly linear

s(bmi) and s(age) edf = 1.00 — essentially linear, no nonlinearity detected

MARS

set.seed(67)

mars_grid <- expand.grid(degree = 1:3, 
                         nprune = 2:15)

mars_fit <- train(log_PFS ~ .,
                 data = training_data,
                 method = "earth",
                 tuneGrid = mars_grid,
                 trControl = ctrl1)
ggplot(mars_fit)

mars_fit$bestTune
##    nprune degree
## 12     13      1
coef(mars_fit$finalModel) 
##       (Intercept)  h(8-lymph_nodes)               ER1        h(39-Ki67) 
##        6.82771514        0.07608370        0.20621038        0.00927652 
##       h(23.1-bmi)             HER21 h(tumor_size-1.3) h(1.3-tumor_size) 
##       -0.04137561       -0.15130797       10.81207684       -5.26460543 
## hormonal_therapy1       h(bmi-35.7) h(tumor_size-0.4) h(tumor_size-1.6) 
##        0.14631728       -0.18303113       -6.10903363        1.24255203 
## h(tumor_size-1.4) 
##       -5.97978991

  • degree = 1, no interactions needed

  • optimal at nprune = 11

hinge functions: - h(8 - lymph_nodes) — effect of lymph nodes below 8

  • h(tumor_size - 1.3) and h(1.3 - tumor_size) together — piecewise effect around tumor size = 1.3cm, a knot/breakpoint

  • h(39 - Ki67) — effect of Ki67 below 39%

  • h(23.1 - bmi) and h(bmi - 35.7) — nonlinear BMI effects at two breakpoints

  • ER1, HER21, hormonal_therapy1 — linear terms

  • h(tumor_size - 0.4) — another tumor size hinge

MARS confirms nonlinear effects for Ki67 and tumor size, consistent with the GAM findings.

resamples

set.seed(67)

resamp <- resamples(list(enet = enet_fit, ols = ols_fit, lasso = lasso_fit, rdige = ridge_fit, gam = gam_fit, mars = mars_fit))

summary(resamp)
## 
## Call:
## summary.resamples(object = resamp)
## 
## Models: enet, ols, lasso, rdige, gam, mars 
## Number of resamples: 10 
## 
## MAE 
##            Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. NA's
## enet  0.5382907 0.5540677 0.5673095 0.5651641 0.5754018 0.5850183    0
## ols   0.5337281 0.5530479 0.5685322 0.5657648 0.5746613 0.5938914    0
## lasso 0.5382907 0.5540677 0.5673095 0.5651641 0.5754018 0.5850183    0
## rdige 0.5385195 0.5544932 0.5677313 0.5655752 0.5756389 0.5858850    0
## gam   0.5402857 0.5560489 0.5643286 0.5648538 0.5733396 0.5906677    0
## mars  0.5419318 0.5617246 0.5668176 0.5680467 0.5754304 0.5966667    0
## 
## RMSE 
##            Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. NA's
## enet  0.6777963 0.6867489 0.7038638 0.7069183 0.7278563 0.7396059    0
## ols   0.6772553 0.6902241 0.7049909 0.7095562 0.7316584 0.7465207    0
## lasso 0.6777963 0.6867489 0.7038638 0.7069183 0.7278563 0.7396059    0
## rdige 0.6790934 0.6880983 0.7073573 0.7087951 0.7295680 0.7422544    0
## gam   0.6748032 0.6884442 0.7006600 0.7064651 0.7285618 0.7444308    0
## mars  0.6799642 0.6914314 0.7012848 0.7092797 0.7292769 0.7458848    0
## 
## Rsquared 
##            Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. NA's
## enet  0.1352050 0.1796566 0.1979205 0.1991726 0.2161934 0.2560116    0
## ols   0.1317340 0.1729421 0.1931844 0.1934841 0.2090219 0.2566592    0
## lasso 0.1352050 0.1796566 0.1979205 0.1991726 0.2161934 0.2560116    0
## rdige 0.1301077 0.1798126 0.1966833 0.1947715 0.2039399 0.2551335    0
## gam   0.1421973 0.1726076 0.2074321 0.2010715 0.2224894 0.2508943    0
## mars  0.1335032 0.1602571 0.1985899 0.1960338 0.2347478 0.2478336    0
parallelplot(resamp, metric = "RMSE")

bwplot(resamp, metric = "RMSE")

GAM has lowest median CV RMSE, followed by enet and lasso. All models overlap, differences are not significant. enet has one outlier fold and performed identically to LASSO, indicating the ridge component didn’t change much. GAM captures the non-linear effects of Ki67 and tumor size, could potentially the best model. However, GAM is less interpretable, and the goal of the project is to understand how demographic, tumor, and treatment characteristics influence progression-free survival in breast cancer patients.

no model is significantly better than any other,the differences in RMSE are not statistically significant. We will decide to go with LASSO over enet, since it only has 1 tuning parameter, no outliers and identical RMSE to enet.

prediction

lasso_pred <- predict(lasso_fit, newdata = testing_data)

# test RMSE
sqrt(mean((lasso_pred - testing_data$log_PFS)^2))
## [1] 0.6841792
#R^2
cor(lasso_pred, testing_data$log_PFS)^2
## [1] 0.1900497

test RMSE = 0.68, slightly lower than CV RMSE = 0.706.

  • 6 models trained and compared via 10-fold CV

  • No significant differences between models

  • Lasso selected as final model due to parsimony and interpretability

  • Test RMSE = 0.684

  • lasso model explains about 19% of variance in log PFS on the test set, consistent with the training CV R^2 of 0.20, which is a good sign that the model is not overfitting.

  • final model: Lasso with lambda = 0.015, CV RMSE = 0.707, Test RMSE = 0.684, test R^2 = 0.190

Part II

load("datB.RData")

datB <- datB |> 
  select(-id) |> 
  mutate(
    menopause = as.factor(menopause), # 0 = Pre-menopausal, 1 = Post-menopausal
    race = as.factor(race), # 1 = White, 2 = Black, 3 = Asian, 4 = Hispanic 
    tumor_grade = as.factor(tumor_grade), # 1 = Well differentiated, 2 = Moderately differentiated, 3 = Poorly differentiated
    ER = as.factor(ER), # 0 = Negative, 1 = Positive 
    PR = as.factor(PR), # 0 = Negative, 1 = Positive
    HER2 = as.factor(HER2), # 0 = Negative, 1 = Positive
    stage = as.factor(stage), # 1 = Stage I, 2 = Stage II, 3 = Stage III
    surgery = as.factor(surgery), # 0 = Breast-conserving surgery, 1 = Mastectomy
    chemo = as.factor(chemo), # 0 = No, 1 = Yes
    hormonal_therapy = as.factor(hormonal_therapy) # 0 = No, 1 = Yes
  )

# predict on datB
lasso_datB <- predict(lasso_fit, newdata = datB)

# RMSE on datB
sqrt(mean((lasso_datB - datB$log_PFS)^2))
## [1] 0.829317
#R^2
cor(lasso_datB, datB$log_PFS)^2
## [1] 0.1565149

  • RMSE increased substantially from 0.684 to 0.829, about a 21% increase in prediction error

  • R^2 dropped from 0.190 to 0.157, explaining even less variance in the new cohort

# Part II - datB analysis

# train/test split
set.seed(67)
data_splitB <- initial_split(datB, prop = 0.8)
training_dataB <- training(data_splitB)
testing_dataB <- testing(data_splitB)

# CV control
ctrl1 <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10, savePredictions = TRUE)

# OLS
set.seed(67)
ols_fitB <- train(log_PFS ~ ., data = training_dataB, method = "lm", trControl = ctrl1)

# Ridge
set.seed(67)
ridge_fitB <- train(log_PFS ~ ., data = training_dataB, method = "glmnet",
                    tuneGrid = expand.grid(alpha = 0, lambda = exp(seq(6, -4, length = 100))),
                    trControl = ctrl1)
## Warning in nominalTrainWorkflow(x = x, y = y, wts = weights, info = trainInfo,
## : There were missing values in resampled performance measures.
# Lasso
set.seed(67)
lasso_fitB <- train(log_PFS ~ ., data = training_dataB, method = "glmnet",
                    tuneGrid = expand.grid(alpha = 1, lambda = exp(seq(4, -10, length = 100))),
                    trControl = ctrl1)
## Warning in nominalTrainWorkflow(x = x, y = y, wts = weights, info = trainInfo,
## : There were missing values in resampled performance measures.
plot(lasso_fitB, xTrans = log)

# Elastic net
set.seed(67)
enet_fitB <- train(log_PFS ~ ., data = training_dataB, method = "glmnet",
                   tuneGrid = expand.grid(alpha = seq(0, 1, length = 21),
                                          lambda = exp(seq(4, -10, length = 100))),
                   trControl = ctrl1)
## Warning in nominalTrainWorkflow(x = x, y = y, wts = weights, info = trainInfo,
## : There were missing values in resampled performance measures.
# GAM
set.seed(67)
gam_fitB <- train(log_PFS ~ ., data = training_dataB, method = "gam", trControl = ctrl1)

# MARS
set.seed(67)
mars_fitB <- train(log_PFS ~ ., data = training_dataB, method = "earth",
                   tuneGrid = mars_grid, trControl = ctrl1)

# Model comparison
resampB <- resamples(list(ols = ols_fitB, ridge = ridge_fitB, lasso = lasso_fitB,
                          enet = enet_fitB, gam = gam_fitB, mars = mars_fitB))
summary(resampB)
## 
## Call:
## summary.resamples(object = resampB)
## 
## Models: ols, ridge, lasso, enet, gam, mars 
## Number of resamples: 10 
## 
## MAE 
##            Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. NA's
## ols   0.5022711 0.5122949 0.5374245 0.5396088 0.5650435 0.5850137    0
## ridge 0.5032439 0.5128698 0.5367535 0.5382015 0.5591122 0.5764579    0
## lasso 0.5004316 0.5123126 0.5367669 0.5385622 0.5629375 0.5807758    0
## enet  0.5031558 0.5127948 0.5367685 0.5381869 0.5592506 0.5766371    0
## gam   0.5100143 0.5206864 0.5371817 0.5390284 0.5551839 0.5724727    0
## mars  0.5083307 0.5190839 0.5352303 0.5410699 0.5591859 0.5858931    0
## 
## RMSE 
##            Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. NA's
## ols   0.6346814 0.6477027 0.6723033 0.6756284 0.7066702 0.7226100    0
## ridge 0.6314345 0.6476131 0.6732795 0.6736970 0.7023192 0.7119490    0
## lasso 0.6323501 0.6458729 0.6737030 0.6748663 0.7047405 0.7179010    0
## enet  0.6314685 0.6475645 0.6732011 0.6736948 0.7024086 0.7121477    0
## gam   0.6340774 0.6513829 0.6717226 0.6731309 0.6965813 0.7075904    0
## mars  0.6366343 0.6458860 0.6728928 0.6741751 0.6973475 0.7201538    0
## 
## Rsquared 
##             Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. NA's
## ols   0.07067034 0.1514811 0.1684969 0.1663007 0.1903439 0.2704946    0
## ridge 0.07568797 0.1575395 0.1703577 0.1705426 0.1903816 0.2786331    0
## lasso 0.07191753 0.1494269 0.1675708 0.1672483 0.1917034 0.2699654    0
## enet  0.07557594 0.1574686 0.1703487 0.1704795 0.1904347 0.2784238    0
## gam   0.09732833 0.1512373 0.1727384 0.1698948 0.1928853 0.2628342    0
## mars  0.07553181 0.1446442 0.1598284 0.1681994 0.1973831 0.2784110    0
bwplot(resampB, metric = "RMSE")

- CV RMSE range is 0.673-0.676, lower than datA 0.706-0.710

  • GAM has the lowest mean RMSE (0.6731) again

  • Ridge and enet are virtually identical (0.6737)

  • All models very close again, we choose to compare LASSO models based on interpretability & consistency

New LASSO prediction part II

set.seed(67)
# predict on datB test set
lasso_predB <- predict(lasso_fitB, newdata = testing_dataB)

# test RMSE
sqrt(mean((lasso_predB - testing_dataB$log_PFS)^2))
## [1] 0.6673874
# R^2
cor(lasso_predB, testing_dataB$log_PFS)^2
## [1] 0.184277
coef(lasso_fitB$finalModel, s = lasso_fitB$bestTune$lambda)
## 20 x 1 sparse Matrix of class "dgCMatrix"
##                   s=0.006406097
## (Intercept)         3.072693034
## age                -0.001056893
## menopause1         -0.030385590
## race2              -0.016998229
## race3               .          
## race4               0.039220631
## bmi                -0.006005407
## tumor_size         -0.045470355
## tumor_grade2        0.079335320
## tumor_grade3       -0.113867622
## lymph_nodes        -0.029558059
## ER1                 0.152841082
## PR1                 0.032199936
## HER21               0.026412723
## Ki67               -0.003987201
## stage2              .          
## stage3             -0.031408696
## surgery1           -0.035872647
## chemo1             -0.006697300
## hormonal_therapy1   0.202269382

coefficient comparison

# extract coefficients from both models
coef_A <- coef(lasso_fit$finalModel, s = lasso_fit$bestTune$lambda) |> 
  as.matrix() |> 
  as.data.frame()

coef_B <- coef(lasso_fitB$finalModel, s = lasso_fitB$bestTune$lambda) |> 
  as.matrix() |> 
  as.data.frame()

# combine into one table
coef_table <- cbind(coef_A, coef_B)
colnames(coef_table) <- c("datA (1990-95)", "datB (2000-05)")

coef_table
##                   datA (1990-95) datB (2000-05)
## (Intercept)          2.600416237    3.072693034
## age                 -0.001738541   -0.001056893
## menopause1           0.000000000   -0.030385590
## race2                0.000000000   -0.016998229
## race3                0.005695736    0.000000000
## race4               -0.024402590    0.039220631
## bmi                  0.000000000   -0.006005407
## tumor_size          -0.047114496   -0.045470355
## tumor_grade2         0.000000000    0.079335320
## tumor_grade3        -0.104548373   -0.113867622
## lymph_nodes         -0.062144518   -0.029558059
## ER1                  0.208395550    0.152841082
## PR1                  0.000000000    0.032199936
## HER21               -0.128160939    0.026412723
## Ki67                -0.005143791   -0.003987201
## stage2               0.015762828    0.000000000
## stage3               0.000000000   -0.031408696
## surgery1             0.000000000   -0.035872647
## chemo1               0.000000000   -0.006697300
## hormonal_therapy1    0.136572696    0.202269382
kable(coef_table, digits = 3, caption = "Table 1. Lasso coefficients from datA and datB models")
Table 1. Lasso coefficients from datA and datB models
datA (1990-95) datB (2000-05)
(Intercept) 2.600 3.073
age -0.002 -0.001
menopause1 0.000 -0.030
race2 0.000 -0.017
race3 0.006 0.000
race4 -0.024 0.039
bmi 0.000 -0.006
tumor_size -0.047 -0.045
tumor_grade2 0.000 0.079
tumor_grade3 -0.105 -0.114
lymph_nodes -0.062 -0.030
ER1 0.208 0.153
PR1 0.000 0.032
HER21 -0.128 0.026
Ki67 -0.005 -0.004
stage2 0.016 0.000
stage3 0.000 -0.031
surgery1 0.000 -0.036
chemo1 0.000 -0.007
hormonal_therapy1 0.137 0.202

Changes - HER2 flipped from negative to positive. In the 1990s cohort HER2 positive was associated with worse PFS, but in the 2000s cohort it became slightly positive. HER2+ is no longer a poor prognostic factor.

  • Hormonal therapy effect strengthened: coefficient grew from 0.134 to 0.202, suggesting hormonal therapy became more effective or more appropriately prescribed.

  • Lymph nodes effect weakened — from -0.062 to -0.030, potentially better treatments

  • More variables retained in datB — suggesting the later cohort has more complex predictive relationships.
